entry.title: Combined summary - Hourglass V2 Update
entry.summary: The Hourglass proposal has been revised to address concerns around the mass liquidation of Pay-to-PubKey (P2PK) funds within the Bitcoin network. The updated version aims to bolster security and stability by limiting the amount of Bitcoin that can be spent from P2PK outputs to a maximum of one Bitcoin per block. This adjustment seeks to prevent market disruptions that could stem from large, simultaneous liquidations. Details of the revised Hourglass proposal, including technical adjustments and the rationale behind these changes, are documented and available for review at [Hourglass Proposal V2](https://github.com/cryptoquick/bips/blob/hourglass-v2/bip-hourglass-v2.mediawiki). This initiative reflects the community's commitment to refining Bitcoin's operational framework in response to identified vulnerabilities, as evidenced by the extensive discussion and feedback process documented in the initial Bitcoin Development Mailing List Discussion, accessible at [Bitcoin Development Mailing List Discussion](https://groups.google.com/g/bitcoindev/c/zmg3U117aNc/m/lDCMs9j7EAAJ). Critics of the proposal argue that imposing restrictions on how individuals can spend their Bitcoins contradicts the core principles of Bitcoin as an unregulated, decentralized peer-to-peer electronic cash system. They emphasize the importance of preserving Bitcoin's foundational attributes to maintain its integrity and utility. This perspective highlights the ongoing debate regarding the balance between innovation and adherence to Bitcoin's original vision. The proposal not only addresses the potential for quantum attacks on P2PK coins but also proposes adjustments to Bitcoin's reward distribution mechanism as a preventive measure. By aligning the issuance of new coins with post-quantum security measures and reducing the daily introduction of bitcoins through block rewards, the proposal aims to mitigate incentives for quantum attacks. It suggests tying the release of P2PK coins directly to the coinbase reward structure, ensuring a controlled and proportional introduction of new coins. However, concerns have been raised about the potential for manipulation by large miners, underscoring the need for careful consideration of any adjustments to consensus rules to prevent exploitation while adhering to cryptocurrency issuance principles. Garlo Nicon's contribution to the discussion emphasizes the strategic importance of setting a specific transaction value, such as 1 BTC, as a standard to simplify the understanding and management of quantum threats. This approach aims to balance the urgency of protecting P2PK coins against the time required for original keyholders to secure their assets, thereby extending the liquidation period and reducing the risk of unauthorized access by quantum hackers. Nicon's insights reflect a broader emphasis on preemptive strategies for cryptocurrency security in anticipation of quantum computing advancements. Finally, there is a clarification that the proposed spending limit applies only to legacy P2PK addresses. Users are encouraged to migrate their funds to modern SegWit/Taproot addresses, which are not subject to these restrictions, highlighting a path forward that respects both security concerns and user autonomy. This guidance underscores the flexibility within the Bitcoin ecosystem to adapt to emerging challenges while offering options to users who prioritize different aspects of their interaction with the network.
